title: Performance Profiling Part 3: Frontend Profiling with Lighthouse and Chrome DevTools
summary: A frontend profiling workflow for production builds using Lighthouse, Chrome DevTools, Core Web Vitals, the Network tab, and React profiling tools.
body:
# Frontend Profiling with Lighthouse and Chrome DevTools

Frontend profiling is different from backend profiling because the runtime environment is unpredictable. Users have different devices, browsers, screen sizes, extensions, CPUs, memory limits, and network conditions.

That is why frontend performance should be measured as user-perceived performance, not just local developer speed.

## Important Frontend Metrics

Lighthouse and browser tooling expose several useful metrics:

- LCP measures when the largest visible content element loads
- CLS measures unexpected layout shift
- INP measures responsiveness to user interactions
- TTI estimates when the page becomes reliably interactive

These metrics are useful because they describe what the user feels: waiting, shifting, lag, and blocked interaction.

## Test Production Builds

Development builds are slower and include extra tooling. Always profile a production build when judging real performance.

```bash
npm run build
npm run preview
```

Use the equivalent preview command for your framework. The point is to profile optimized assets, not the development server.

## Use Incognito Mode

Browser extensions can affect measurements. Incognito mode reduces noise and helps make repeated Lighthouse runs more comparable.

Also test with throttling. A page that feels instant on a high-end laptop may struggle on a mid-range phone over a slow network.

## Running Lighthouse

Lighthouse gives a broad first pass. It can point out large images, render-blocking resources, unused JavaScript, layout shifts, and slow main-thread work.

Treat Lighthouse as a starting point. It tells you where to look, but Chrome DevTools helps you understand why the problem happens.

## Chrome DevTools Performance Tab

The Performance tab records loading, scripting, rendering, painting, and user interactions. Look for long tasks, expensive script execution, repeated layout work, and delayed rendering.

If the main thread is busy for long periods, the page cannot respond quickly to input.

## Network Waterfalls

The Network tab shows request order, size, timing, and blocking. It is useful for finding waterfalls where one request unnecessarily waits for another.

Common problems include large JavaScript bundles, unoptimized images, slow fonts, missing compression, and API calls that block initial rendering.

## React Profiler and React Scan

For React applications, unnecessary renders can create real interaction cost. The React Profiler helps identify which components rendered and how long they took.

React Scan can help spot components that rerender more often than expected. Use these tools after browser-level profiling shows that rendering work is part of the bottleneck.

Frontend profiling works best when you combine metrics, browser traces, network inspection, and framework-specific tools.
